question_asked: Think back to packing for Roskilde, what did you bring, and where did most of it come from, new, borrowed, or stuff you already had?
answer_text: I actually didn't bring a tent or a sleeping mat or anything of sleeping bag. I literally just brought like food, clothing stuff. So very, very little. I'm sleeping in the tent of a friend. So I'm very lightweight.

question_asked: When the festival wraps up, what are you planning to take home, and what'll you leave here? Be honest;)
answer_text: Yeah, I'm actually bringing everything of what I brought here also back. Only at the festival I will buy cans and stuff, so all the beer that I consume and also a plastic bag, I'm holding one just in my hand. That stuff I will for sure leave here. Food-wise, maybe napkins that I buy with my food and packaging, but no infrastructure stuff. None of that I will leave here. I didn't bring it in the first place.

question_asked: Do you think waste is a personal or festival responsibility?
answer_text: Very much a festival responsibility of both. I guess there is a big part on the festival for providing infrastructure and incentivizing a culture around sustainability. But then often individuals try to go the easiest way. So individuals also by regulation from the festivals that needs to be nudged in the right direction. And I personally, for instance, also often have the difficulty. And if I'm in the middle of a party, not seeing a trash can. And there, then I need to throw my beer can on the ground, spit my chewing gum on the ground, little things like that. At least for infrastructure stuff like tents and sleeping mats, I've never left anything behind or made a bad experience myself with that.

question_asked: So you've never left gear behind, but have you ever seen others do it and wondered why?
answer_text: I actually have never seen others leave gear behind. I have only heard that now with the Roskilde Festival. That's apparently that bad. But I'm also not really a festival goer. So gear behind was not really before this festival here. A topic that I heard being a problem, I always of course knew about like normal garbage, like plastic bags and cans and stuff. But gear, I didn't know that people actually like used one-way gear to support the first festival. It broke during the festival and they left it and that was intentional. That was quite new to me and also quite shocking, being honest.

question_asked: Oh wow, that’s surprising. What do you think the festival could do to stop that from happening?
answer_text: I mean, I'm aware that Roskilde Festival is already regulating to certain types of tents, not being broad, the type of one-way tent that is a one-way tent. Otherwise, I guess they could request subsidies from state organizations for buying themselves camping gear and renting it out. Especially if there's kind of a collaboration of multiple festivals, the camping gear could be used over the whole summer. So every camp goer doesn't need to bring their own stuff. Also, they could provide it already built up. Yes, that comes at a higher cost of personal effort, like personnel, so people working at the festival. But I was always a big fan of glamping, glamorous camping. And already have a bit of a nicer venue where I go to, not too dirty of a camp, and then everything is also taken care of. So that seems a bit more comfortable and will for sure be more sustainable in less one way. Won't be that great for this amount of people, like 90,000 people, but worth considering nevertheless.

question_asked: Any other ideas you think would help with the gear waste?
answer_text: Maybe also Roskilde could go into collaboration with gear rental or outdoor stores in general. I haven't seen a single outdoor store for gear here on the campground, even though it's a big topic. So why doesn't Decathlon help the festival to organize their stuff? And get people an accommodation. And possibly also people in the city of Roskilde, couldn't they also host some people? Like as I understood, the big topic is tents and sleeping beds. Aren't there enough couch surfing opportunities somewhere around? If it's really only around the infrastructure, that would be okay. And by a building, like in Dream City, I've heard people have a year ahead of time access. Can they just build pavilions where people can camp underneath? At least some enough. So you don't need to have a one-way pavilion. That would be open topics and open ideas.

question_asked: Do you think waste is a personal or festival responsibility?
answer_text: No.

question_asked: Do you think waste is a personal or festival responsibility?
answer_text: I think first and foremost, personal responsibility. But I do think that as a festival, you need to understand that stuff happens. Not everyone feels that same responsibility. So there definitely needs to be measures in place to make sure that after a festival, everything is left as it was before.

question_asked: When the festival wraps up, what are you planning to take home, and what'll you leave here? Be honest;)
answer_text: Well, obviously I'm going to try to take everything with me. I mean, there's always some trash that gets left behind, but yeah, maybe some like degradable, biodegradable food.

question_asked: Personal responsibility first,  love that. What's one thing Roskilde could do to make it easier for people to actually take their gear home?
answer_text: I mean, a lot of different ways you can go about answering this question. Of course, you can include some type of incentive. But you can also, what's the word for the opposite when it's like a punishment based thing? So like if you leave something behind, you know, there's like a consequence. Or maybe like sorting boxes, some place that people can throw it and doesn't, they don't have to like carry it home, something like that. I don't know.

question_asked: Sorting boxes idea is interesting,  what would make you actually use one rather than just leaving stuff at your pitch?
answer_text: Well, I don't think anyone wants to purposely litter or leave stuff behind. I just think sometimes after like a five day, one week festival, like the idea of carrying a tent home just feels like too much of a burden. So I think if there are sorting boxes, you just offer like an easy solution for people to end. Not have to carry all their stuff, but also not be horrible and litter.

question_asked: So basically it's about making doing the right thing feel effortless,  is there anything else about this you think we're missing?
answer_text: No.

question_asked: When the festival wraps up, what are you planning to take home, and what'll you leave here? Be honest;)
answer_text: We'll bring home everything. We've done that for a lot of years. We rented our pavilions. Pavilions? I don't know. Yeah, pavilions. And, yeah. We'll bring everything, like, not our trash, but we have, you know, bags for that. We live in Leave No Trace, and we have bags for plastic and food and stuff like that. So, yeah, we'll not bring that home, but everything else, oh. Oh, is it still recording? Okay, I hope you got my answer.

question_asked: Do you think waste is a personal or festival responsibility?
answer_text: Both, definitely. I think Roskilde has very good opportunities to, you know, get picked up or take good transportation. So it's easy to carry your stuff just outside of the camping area and then get on some kind of public transport and then get home or get picked up by a taxi or a parent if you're lucky like that. Yeah, so I think it's both. It's our responsibility to take care of our stuff and it's the festival's responsibility to make sure that we are able to bring things home after a week of paddling.

question_asked: Think back to packing for Roskilde, what did you bring, and where did most of it come from, new, borrowed, or stuff you already had?
answer_text: Most of my things was stuff that I had been collecting through the years. This is my 10th year at Røskilde, so I'm experienced. Yeah, so I know how to take my stuff home and then... I bring my clothes and my gear. My chair is from last year and the year before that. And the tent is new, but we've been going with the same tent for lots of years. So it was time for a new one. Yeah, and that's, yeah.

question_asked: Love that "week of paddling" image,  so what's the one thing the festival could do to make it even easier for first-timers to bring everything home?
answer_text: Yeah, that's a good question, but maybe like some like shuffle, small shuffle buses into the camping area. So, yeah, so if you live in the middle of everything, you don't have to walk a long way to go to like the outside of the camping site.

question_asked: Shuttle buses into the camp itself,  smart. Have you ever seen someone near you leave gear behind, and what did they say about it?
answer_text: No, we've been living in Leave No Trace for five years at this point. And before that we lived in Clean Out Loud. So, yeah, but, you know, in the P, where all the teenagers live, there's a lot of people leaving stuff behind. But not where we stay because we actively seeked and wanted to live somewhere where we brought everything home.

question_asked: Love that,  "infrastructure to make it realistic." What's one thing that would've actually made it easier for you to pack out your stuff today?

question_asked: Clearer sorting makes sense,  have you actually spotted any of those containers this trip, or did they kind of blend into the chaos?

question_asked: Anything else about the waste setup here that Roskilde should know?

question_asked: Think back to packing for Roskilde, what did you bring, and where did most of it come from, new, borrowed, or stuff you already had?

question_asked: Do you think waste is a personal or festival responsibility?

question_asked: When the festival wraps up, what are you planning to take home, and what'll you leave here? Be honest;)

question_asked: So guests carry most of it,  what's the hardest part of actually packing up and hauling everything out?
answer_text: The buses that won't allow big packs so when you have a wagon or stuff on wheels you can't bring it into the buses. Which is weird when you brought it out here. You should be able to get it home as well.

question_asked: That bus rule sounds genuinely frustrating,  has that ever actually stopped you from taking something home?
answer_text: Yes, last year I left a full wagon of all sorts of crap due to the bus saying that I was not allowed to bring it on.

question_asked: That wagon story is a perfect example,  is there anything else Roskilde could do to make it easier to actually get your stuff home?
answer_text: Yes.

question_asked: Do you think waste is a personal or festival responsibility?
answer_text: I think it's definitely both, right? I mean, of course I have a personal responsibility for my waste, but since the festival provides the infrastructure, they should also support me in doing the best possible choice.

question_asked: Think back to packing for Roskilde, what did you bring, and where did most of it come from, new, borrowed, or stuff you already had?
answer_text: Yeah, so for this Roskilde Festival I brought a tent, a sleeping mat and also a sleeping bag. A lot of it was something that I actually already have or either borrowed.

question_asked: When the festival wraps up, what are you planning to take home, and what'll you leave here? Be honest;)
answer_text: I will take all of it home. Super, very nice gear that I have with me. So, shouldn't go to waste. I really hope that people will not jump my tent, as they sometimes do.

question_asked: Love that framing of "support me in doing the best possible choice",  what's one thing Roskilde could actually do to make that easier?
answer_text: I do think what they're already doing right now to make me be able to rent my stuff is super nice. So I think they should definitely keep that path going. We'd love to stay in Glambington as well.

question_asked: Glambington sounds like the dream! What's the one thing stopping you from booking it next time?
answer_text: It's definitely a bit expensive, so that's why I'm not gonna do that. But maybe if I make a fortune selling Bradley, I should definitely consider doing the glamping sun.

question_asked: Anything else about gear or waste at Roskilde that you think people don't talk about enough?
answer_text: Maybe they don't talk enough about the fact that actually you rely on a lot of cheap labor from a lot of people going around cleaning. So a lot of people don't think it's their responsibility when you just have homeless people collecting cans for them. It's almost like a luxury festival.

question_asked: When the festival wraps up, what are you planning to take home, and what'll you leave here? Be honest;)
answer_text: I only plan on bringing home my tent, my sleeping bag, my mattress and the clothes that I bring. That's pretty much the only solid things that I plan on bringing home. But other than that, all the rest of the stuff is consumable.

question_asked: Think back to packing for Roskilde, what did you bring, and where did most of it come from, new, borrowed, or stuff you already had?
answer_text: I packed the tent and I packed sleeping bags and all the big stuff, something I had beforehand. And then all the more usable stuff, like the more consumable stuff, something I bought out in the city. So I bought a sprayer for the camp. And I bought a tarp. And I bought food. Different places. And I always seem to lose my football. So I also bought that. Well, I actually didn't this time, but usually I do.

question_asked: Do you think waste is a personal or festival responsibility?
answer_text: 100% a festival responsibility because I wouldn't be buying this stuff without the festival. So in my mind... Bye, bye, Mr. Man. American buy. Rah, rah, rah. No, festival responsibility definitely. Because I wouldn't have all the... I wouldn't have all the things that I have today if I hadn't planned for going on a festival. So definitely festival.

question_asked: Since you see it as the festival's responsibility,  what's one thing Roskilde could actually do to make that easier for you?
answer_text: Garbage sorting, I think, at the end of the festival. Kind of like they already do, but that's definitely the right direction because I would like to just hand in all the trash that I have for them to collect because otherwise I'll just throw it out elsewhere. So if they sort it and they collect it and they dispose of it in a proper way, then I think it's the most responsible way to take care of it.

question_asked: So when you picture "handing it in",  is there a specific spot or moment you'd want that to happen?
answer_text: I think it should be the last Saturday or the Sunday after the music because that's when people leave. And that should be it. Bye, bye, Mr. Include that in the results, please.

question_asked: Anything else about the gear or waste situation here that you think people don't talk about enough?
answer_text: I think people should be able to buy stuff that was left behind from last, like previous years. So that if I had to go on Roskilde Festival, I would be able to buy a tent that was left last year for a very favorable price. I think that would be good.

question_asked: Think back to packing for Roskilde, what did you bring, and where did most of it come from, new, borrowed, or stuff you already had?
answer_text: Most of my things came from stuff that I already had and that I've used before. Some things I had to upgrade because it was broken, so I had to buy new stuff. But I would only buy it if I needed new stuff or I borrow stuff.

question_asked: When the festival wraps up, what are you planning to take home, and what'll you leave here? Be honest;)
answer_text: I'm bringing everything home. There's nothing that I would leave here. Yeah, I reuse all of the things I have and yeah.

question_asked: Do you think waste is a personal or festival responsibility?
answer_text: I think it's both. I think it's great that they have, they give the opportunity or the possibility to rent tents and mattresses in Pavlionga. I think that's really great. But I also think that it is a personal responsibility to some certain extent. I think that every person who comes here has, you know, time to figure out what to bring, how to bring it, borrow stuff, reuse things, take them back home, even though that you don't want to on the last day. But I think that it's, yeah, also a personal responsibility. But I mean, I've seen that, you know, get a tent and renting mattresses and stuff has been sold out. So maybe if they made it even, you know, even more accessible or had even more stuff to rent, that could also be great.

question_asked: You mentioned the rental stuff selling out,  what would make you actually book it in advance next time?
answer_text: I mean, just being accessible, actually, or the possibility for it to be an opportunity to book because it was sold out. Yeah. Because I did check pretty early on, but it was pretty quickly that it disappeared. But I don't know, maybe just be even better in time. I didn't even know that I was going to be camping here until like a month ago. But yeah. Yeah, I've been reusing myself for such a long time. So, yeah.

question_asked: Since you only knew a month out, how did you actually sort your gear that fast?
answer_text: Because I've already had stuff for the past, I've been reusing my things for the past 15 years. So again, it's only the stuff that really breaks and I need new things for. Yeah, that I would buy or borrow.

question_asked: 15 years of reuse is impressive! Is there anything the festival could do that would actually make a difference to how people handle their gear?

question_asked: Anything else you'd want the festival to know about this?

question_asked: Do you think waste is a personal or festival responsibility?

question_asked: Think back to packing for Roskilde, what did you bring, and where did most of it come from, new, borrowed, or stuff you already had?
answer_text: We have a tent that we also used last year, which we actually really wanted to reuse. And my friend reused her mattress that she bought, well, rented from Roskilde Festival last year. We also stay clean out loud, so we try at least to clean up after ourselves and sort our trash. question_asked: What kind of opportunity or setup do you think would actually make people bring their tent home?

question_asked: You mentioned "expensive tents",  do you think price is really what stops people, or is it more the hassle of packing up wet gear?
answer_text: I think it's both. I guess if the last day is really, really a shitty day with bad weather, people would more likely leave their stuff. But if people have invested in good quality tents, I think they would put more effort into bringing it back home and reusing it year after year.

question_asked: Is there anything else Roskilde could do that we haven't touched on yet?

answer_text: I'll tell my brother that I had a more, a better, more amazing time than him.

question_asked: Haha, fair. So what do you actually think happens to the tent and chairs you leave?
answer_text: Somebody clean them up. Not my problem. Somebody will clean them up.

question_asked: Anything else you'd want the festival to know about this?
answer_text: No!

question_asked: Think back to packing for Roskilde, what did you bring, and where did most of it come from, new, borrowed, or stuff you already had?
answer_text: This is us basically flipping through the... I brought a tent and a sleeping bag and a mattress and it was stuff I already had.

question_asked: Do you think waste is a personal or festival responsibility?
answer_text: I think it's a festival responsibility, but also a personal responsibility. So both.

question_asked: When the festival wraps up, what are you planning to take home, and what'll you leave here? Be honest;)
answer_text: I will take everything home that is not damaged. And definitely my sleeping bag and the tent and my clothes.

question_asked: Curious what you mean by "festival responsibility",  what's one thing Roskilde could actually do to make that easier for you?
answer_text: I really like that they make good mattresses and pavilions and tents that you can buy. Then you have a deposit and then you can deliver it back. And then you can get the deposit back.

question_asked: That deposit idea is interesting,  have you ever actually used or seen a scheme like that work somewhere?
answer_text: No, I haven't.

question_asked: Anything else about gear or waste at Roskilde that you think people don't talk about enough?
answer_text: I think the most waste is probably the tents and the pavilions. So maybe I don't think you talk about how many tents that is thrown out afterwards.
